section.title: Immorality in the Church
section.sectionRationale: Paul turns from the divisions to a scandal that the proud Corinthians have tolerated: a man living with his father's wife, an immorality that even the pagans would not accept. Their boasting in the face of such sin is intolerable. Paul commands the church, gathered in the name of the Lord Jesus, to hand the man over to Satan for the destruction of the flesh, that his spirit may be saved. He grounds the call for purity in the gospel itself: Christ our Passover lamb has been sacrificed, so the church must cleanse out the old leaven and keep the feast in sincerity and truth, judging those inside the church while leaving outsiders to God.
section.thesis: The church must not tolerate flagrant, unrepentant sin in its midst but must lovingly and solemnly discipline it, handing the immoral man over to Satan that his spirit may be saved, because Christ our Passover lamb has been sacrificed and His people are called to cleanse out the old leaven and live as a new, unleavened batch of sincerity and truth.
section.frameNote: Three movements: the scandal and the sentence, hand him over to Satan that his spirit may be saved; Christ our Passover and the call to cleanse out the old leaven; and the church's duty to judge those inside while leaving outsiders to God.

units.label: The scandal and the sentence
units.note: hand him over to Satan that his spirit may be saved

verses.text: It is actually reported that there is {{porneia|sexual immorality}} among you, and of a kind that is intolerable even among pagans: A man has his father’s wife.
And you are proud! Shouldn’t you rather have been stricken with grief and have removed from your fellowship the man who did this?
Although I am absent from you in body, I am present with you in spirit, and I have already pronounced judgment on the one who did this, just as if I were present.
When you are assembled in the name of our Lord Jesus and I am with you in spirit, along with the power of the Lord Jesus,
hand this man over to Satan for the destruction of the flesh, so that his spirit may be saved on the Day of the Lord.

units.label: Christ our Passover, cleanse out the leaven
units.note: a little leaven works through the whole batch

verses.text: Your boasting is not good. Do you not know that a little {{zyme|leaven}} works through the whole batch of dough?
{{ekkathairo|Get rid of}} the old leaven, that you may be a new unleavened batch, as you really are. For Christ, our {{pascha|Passover lamb}}, has been sacrificed.
Therefore let us keep the feast, not with the old bread, leavened with malice and wickedness, but with the unleavened bread of sincerity and of truth.

units.label: Judge those inside the church
units.note: expel the wicked man from among you

verses.text: I wrote you in my letter not to associate with sexually immoral people.
I was not including the sexually immoral of this world, or the greedy and swindlers, or idolaters. In that case you would have to leave this world.

But now I am writing you not to associate with anyone who claims to be a brother but is sexually immoral or greedy, an idolater or a verbal abuser, a drunkard or a swindler. With such a man do not even eat.
What business of mine is it to judge those outside the church? Are you not to judge those inside?
God will judge those outside. “Expel the wicked man from among you.”

porneia.gloss: Sexual immorality of every kind, sexual activity outside the marriage of one man and one woman. Here it names an incestuous union so shocking that even pagan society condemned it. The word covers a broad range of sexual sin and appears repeatedly in 1 Corinthians as a chief mark of the old life that the gospel is meant to leave behind.

zyme.gloss: Leaven or yeast, a small piece of fermented dough kneaded into a new batch to make it rise. It pictures how a hidden influence spreads silently until it permeates the whole. Paul uses it for tolerated sin: a little of it, left unaddressed, works through the entire congregation, which is why the old leaven must be cleaned out.

ekkathairo.gloss: To clean out thoroughly, to purge completely. The image is of scouring a house of every trace of leaven before Passover. Paul commands the church to cleanse out the old leaven of sin so that it may be in practice what it already is in Christ, a new unleavened batch, holy and set apart.

pascha.gloss: The Passover, and the Passover lamb whose blood spared Israel from death in Egypt. Paul declares that Christ is our Passover lamb, already sacrificed. As Israel purged all leaven from their homes at Passover, so the church, redeemed by the blood of the true Lamb, must purge out the old leaven of sin and keep the feast in sincerity and truth.

basic.context: A man in the Corinthian church was sleeping with his father's wife, a scandal even pagans would not stomach, and instead of grieving, the church was proud. Paul is appalled. He tells them that when they gather in the name of the Lord Jesus, they must remove this man, handing him over to Satan for the destruction of the flesh, with the aim that his spirit may be saved on the last day. He warns that a little tolerated sin spreads like yeast through the whole church. Because Christ our Passover lamb has been sacrificed, the church must clean out the old leaven and live in sincerity and truth. The church is to judge those inside its fellowship, leaving outsiders to God's judgment.
basic.hermeneutics: This is a real case treated as a pattern for church discipline. The severity is aimed at restoration, not mere punishment: the goal stated in verse 5 is that his spirit may be saved. The Passover imagery in verses 6 to 8 grounds the ethics in the gospel: because of what Christ has done, the church must become what it already is.
basic.originalLanguages: The sin is porneia, sexual immorality, here of the most flagrant kind. Tolerated sin is zyme, leaven, a little of which works through the whole batch. So Paul commands them to ekkathairo, to clean out completely, the old leaven. The reason is the pascha: Christ our Passover lamb has been sacrificed, so the redeemed people must purge the leaven and keep the feast.
basic.typology: The Passover of Exodus 12 is fulfilled in Christ, the Lamb whose blood delivers His people from death. The purging of all leaven from Israel's homes before Passover is fulfilled in the church's call to purge out sin. And Paul's closing command, expel the wicked man from among you, draws directly on the covenant refrain of Deuteronomy.
basic.christ: Christ is the true Passover lamb, already sacrificed, whose death both redeems the church and grounds its call to holiness. Discipline is exercised in His name and by His power. The whole logic is gospel-shaped: the Lamb has been slain, therefore the people He has purchased must live as the pure, unleavened batch they now are in Him. Even the severest discipline aims at salvation on the Day of the Lord, the day of Christ's return.
basic.god: God is holy and will not have His people trifle with sin, for a little leaven corrupts the whole. He acts through the gathered church and the power of the Lord Jesus. He is the final judge of those outside, and He is merciful, ordering even discipline toward the saving of the sinner's spirit. His holiness and His mercy meet in the cross of the Passover Lamb.
basic.crossReferences: Exodus 12 gives the Passover lamb and the removal of leaven. Deuteronomy repeatedly commands Israel to purge the evil from among them. Matthew 18 lays out the steps of church discipline aimed at winning the brother. Galatians 5 warns that a little leaven leavens the whole lump.
basic.oneStory: From Egypt God redeemed a people by the blood of the lamb and called them to be holy, purging out all leaven. That story reaches its fulfilment in Christ, the true Passover Lamb, whose blood redeems the church and calls it to be a new, unleavened people. Church discipline is part of God's long work of forming a holy people for Himself, a people set apart by the blood of the Lamb until the Day of the Lord.

context.literary: Chapter 5 opens a new section, moving from divisions to concrete moral failures reported to Paul. The passage runs from the shocking report and Paul's verdict, verses 1 to 5, to the Passover argument for purity, verses 6 to 8, to a clarification of an earlier letter and the command to judge those inside, verses 9 to 13. The section is bracketed by the theme of removing the offender, stated in verse 2 and sealed with the Deuteronomy citation in verse 13.
context.historical: Roman law itself forbade marriage to a stepmother, so the union was scandalous even by pagan standards, which makes the church's pride the more startling. Corinth was notorious for sexual license, and the believers seem to have carried a boastful, permissive spirit into the church. Communal meals and table fellowship were central to church life, so the command not even to eat with such a person was a serious social and spiritual measure.
context.canonical: The passage stands within the Bible's larger teaching on holiness and discipline. It draws on the Passover and the exodus deliverance, on the recurring Deuteronomic command to purge evil from the covenant community, and it coheres with Jesus' instructions on church discipline in Matthew 18 and Paul's parallel warning in Galatians 5:9.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To rebuke the church's proud tolerance of flagrant sin, to command loving but firm discipline that aims at the sinner's ultimate salvation, and to ground the call to purity in the finished work of Christ our Passover, so that the church lives out its true identity as a holy, unleavened people.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The specific case describes one appalling situation, but the principles are prescriptive for the church: do not tolerate unrepentant, flagrant sin among those who claim to be believers, exercise discipline in Christ's name for restoration, and judge those inside the fellowship while leaving outsiders to God. The measures are for professing believers, not a mandate to police the world.

debates.question: What does it mean to 'hand this man over to Satan for the destruction of the flesh, so that his spirit may be saved'?

views.label: Removal from the church into Satan's realm
views.case: Putting the man out of the fellowship removes him from the protection and blessing of the community into the world, Satan's domain, so that the painful consequences of sin, the destruction of the flesh, might bring him to repentance and final salvation.

views.label: A stress on mortification and restoration
views.case: Whatever the exact mechanism, the aim is explicitly redemptive: the destruction of the flesh means the breaking of sinful indulgence, or bodily affliction, ordered toward the saving of his spirit on the Day of the Lord, not his damnation.

debates.bearing: Both orthodox readings agree the purpose is restorative, not vindictive; discipline exists to bring the sinner to repentance and to guard the holiness of the church, and it is exercised in the name and power of the Lord Jesus.

study.originalLanguages: The reported sin is porneia, sexual immorality, here so flagrant that even pagans would recoil. Paul's concern is that tolerated sin behaves like zyme, leaven: a small hidden portion works quietly through the whole batch, so the congregation cannot leave it alone. His command is to ekkathairo, to purge it out completely, as Israel scoured every crumb of leaven from the house before the feast. The ground of the command is the pascha: Christ our Passover lamb has already been sacrificed, so the people bought by His blood must clean out the old leaven and keep the feast with the unleavened bread of sincerity and truth.

typology.fulfillment: Fulfils the Passover lamb of Exodus 12, whose blood spared Israel from death; Christ has been sacrificed as the true Lamb whose blood redeems the church.

typology.type: Purging the leaven before the feast
typology.fulfillment: The removal of all leaven from Israel's homes at Passover is fulfilled in the church's call to cleanse out sin and live as a new, unleavened people of sincerity and truth.

typology.type: Expel the wicked from among you
typology.fulfillment: The recurring Deuteronomic command to purge evil from the covenant community is applied to the church as the new-covenant people who must guard their holiness.

study.christ: Christ is the true Passover Lamb, already sacrificed, and everything in the passage flows from His cross. His blood redeems the church, and precisely because the Lamb has been slain, His people must live as the pure, unleavened batch they already are in Him. The discipline itself is carried out in the name of the Lord Jesus and with His power, not on merely human authority. And the whole aim is fixed on Him: that the sinner's spirit may be saved on the Day of the Lord, the day Christ returns to judge and to gather His own.
study.god: God is holy and jealous for the purity of His people, unwilling to let a little sin corrupt the whole. He works through the gathered assembly and the power of the Lord Jesus to discipline in love. He reserves the judgment of outsiders to Himself, while His people judge those within. And He is merciful even in severity, ordering discipline toward the saving of the sinner's spirit, so that His holiness and His grace meet, as they do supremely at the cross of the Passover Lamb.

crossReferences.note: The Passover lamb whose blood spares Israel from death, fulfilled in Christ our Passover.
Purge the evil from among you, the covenant refrain Paul cites in verse 13.
Jesus' steps of church discipline, aimed at winning the sinning brother.
A little leaven leavens the whole lump, the same warning applied to false teaching.
Likely the restoration of a disciplined offender, showing discipline's redemptive goal, forgiveness and comfort lest he be overwhelmed.

study.oneStory: God redeemed Israel from Egypt by the blood of the lamb and called them to be holy, purging out all leaven. That pattern finds its fulfilment in Christ, the true Passover Lamb, whose blood redeems the church and summons it to be a new, unleavened people. Discipline is not cruelty but part of God's long work of forming a holy people for Himself, guarding the community until the Day of the Lord and seeking always the sinner's restoration. The story runs from the exodus lamb to the risen Christ, gathering a pure people who keep the feast in sincerity and truth.

quiz.q: What was so alarming about the Corinthians' response to the immorality among them?

options.text: Instead of grieving and removing the man, they were proud.
options.note: Verse 2 rebukes their pride where there should have been grief and action.

options.text: They grieved but were slow to act.
options.note: There is no grief in view; Paul says they were proud.

options.text: They quietly ignored the situation.
options.note: Worse than ignoring, they were boasting.

options.text: They immediately expelled the man.
options.note: They had not acted; Paul must command the removal.

quiz.q: What is the stated aim of handing the man over to Satan?

options.text: That his spirit may be saved on the Day of the Lord.
options.note: Verse 5 makes the goal explicitly redemptive.

options.text: To punish him with no thought of restoration.
options.note: Discipline is severe, but its aim is salvation, not mere punishment.

options.text: To protect the church's reputation in the city.
options.note: The concern is holiness and the man's salvation, not public image.

options.text: To condemn him finally and forever.
options.note: The opposite; the goal is that his spirit may be saved.

quiz.q: Why does Paul say a little leaven matters so much?

options.text: Tolerated sin spreads quietly through the whole church.
options.note: Verse 6 warns that a little leaven works through the whole batch.

options.text: Sin is only a problem when it becomes widespread.
options.note: The point is that even a little, left alone, corrupts everything.

options.text: One person's sin never affects the wider church.
options.note: This is exactly what Paul denies with the leaven image.

options.text: Small sins do not really concern God.
options.note: Paul treats the tolerated sin as gravely serious.

quiz.q: How does Paul ground the call to purity in the gospel?

options.text: Christ our Passover lamb has been sacrificed, so cleanse out the old leaven.
options.note: Verse 7 roots the ethic in Christ's finished sacrifice.

options.text: By appealing only to the church's fear of consequences.
options.note: The motive is the cross, not merely fear.

options.text: By pointing to the Corinthians' own good record.
options.note: Their record is the problem; the ground of purity is Christ.

options.text: By arguing that holiness earns salvation.
options.note: Holiness flows from the Lamb already sacrificed, not to earn salvation.

quiz.q: Whom is the church responsible to judge, according to this passage?

options.text: Those inside the church, leaving outsiders to God's judgment.
options.note: Verses 12 and 13 assign inside judgment to the church, outsiders to God.

options.text: Everyone in the surrounding city equally.
options.note: Paul explicitly limits the church's judgment to those inside.

options.text: No one at all, since judging is always wrong.
options.note: Paul commands the church to judge those inside.

options.text: Only the church's leaders, never its members.
options.note: The whole assembly is addressed, not leaders alone.

questions: The Corinthians were proud where they should have grieved. Where might you be treating lightly a sin that ought to grieve you?
Church discipline here aims at saving the sinner, not destroying him. How does that reframe the way you think about confronting sin in the church?
A little leaven works through the whole batch. What small, tolerated compromise in your life or community might be quietly spreading?
Because Christ our Passover has been sacrificed, believers are called to become what they already are. How does the gospel motivate your pursuit of holiness rather than mere rule-keeping?
Paul distinguishes judging those inside the church from judging outsiders. How can you hold real accountability within the church while showing grace to those outside it?
Keeping the feast with sincerity and truth means clearing out malice and wickedness. What old leaven do you most need to let Christ clean out this week?

translationNotes.note: Hand this man over to Satan for the destruction of the flesh, so that his spirit may be saved: the destruction of the flesh means the breaking of sinful indulgence, or bodily affliction, and the explicit aim is his salvation on the last day, not his damnation.
Christ, our Passover lamb, has been sacrificed draws on Exodus 12; as Israel removed all leaven at Passover, the church redeemed by the true Lamb must purge out the old leaven of sin.
Expel the wicked man from among you quotes the recurring command of Deuteronomy to purge evil from the covenant community, now applied to the church as God's new-covenant people.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched from bible.helloao.org for 1 Corinthians 5:1-13; any em dashes in the source have been rendered as commas or semicolons per the no-dash style rule, with wording unchanged.
